hw_1.title: Homework 1
hw_1.description: Introduction to algorithm analysis and complexity
hw_1.problem_preview: Problem 1: Given an array of n integers, analyze the time complexity of your sorting algorithm. Problem 2: Prove that f(n) = n² + 3n + 5 is O(n²).
hint_sequence: Look at the dominant term in the function.
Which term grows fastest as n increases?
Can you find constants c and n₀ to satisfy the definition?
What's the relationship between your constants and the Big O notation?

hw_2.title: Homework 2
hw_2.problem_preview: Problem 1: Design a greedy algorithm for activity selection. Problem 2: Prove the greedy choice property.
hint_sequence: Is there a property that makes a local choice globally optimal?
What happens when you pick the item with the earliest finish time?
Can you prove this choice leads to an optimal solution?
How would you construct the remaining solution after making the greedy choice?

hw_3.title: Homework 3
hw_3.problem_preview: Problem 1: Solve a variant of matrix chain multiplication using DP. Problem 2: Analyze the recursion tree for fibonacci.
hint_sequence: What subproblems are you solving repeatedly?
How can you avoid recomputing the same subproblem?
What is the recurrence relation for this problem?
Can you draw the recursion tree and count the work at each level?
What pattern do you see in the tree structure?

hw_4.title: Homework 4
hw_4.description: Graph algorithms - shortest paths and minimum spanning trees
hw_4.problem_preview: Problem 1: Implement Dijkstra's algorithm and prove correctness. Problem 2: Find the minimum spanning tree using Prim's algorithm.
hint_sequence: What property allows Dijkstra's to work correctly?
In what order should you process the vertices?
Why does the greedy choice (picking the nearest unvisited vertex) give the shortest path?
How can you adapt this algorithm for different graph structures?
What goes wrong if edges can have negative weights?

hw_5.title: Homework 5
hw_5.description: Advanced topics - flow networks, NP-completeness intro
hw_5.problem_preview: Problem 1: Find the maximum flow in a network using Ford-Fulkerson. Problem 2: Prove a problem is NP-complete via reduction.
hint_sequence: What is the maximum amount of flow that can go through each edge?
Can you find a path from source to sink where more flow can be pushed?
What happens when no more augmenting paths exist?
How does the value of the max flow relate to a cut?
Can you show the correspondence between your flow and a certificate of NP-completeness?
